articles.title: Wyoming home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI
articles.dek: Quarterly, annual, and longer-run state HPI comparisons with a clear boundary between market movement and property value.
articles.previewText: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for Wyoming and why an index cannot value a specific property.
articles.metaDescription: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for Wyoming and why an index cannot value a specific property. Review official evidence, comparisons, limitations, and pra

sections.heading: What the latest state index shows
body: The latest evidence for Wyoming puts FHFA purchase-only HPI at 546.3, while quarterly HPI change is -1.9%. The purchase-only index tracks broad price movement in repeat transactions and is designed to show direction over time rather than a typical sale price. Reading the figures together is more useful than treating either as a verdict: the first describes one part of the market and the second supplies context for the borrower decision. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state home-price index evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. This is a planning signal, not a quote, appraisal, approval decision, or forecast, and it should lead to more precise questions rather than a predetermined loan choice.

sections.heading: Quarterly and annual changes use different windows
body: The clearest comparison in this release is quarterly HPI change at -1.9%. The shorter comparison can be more sensitive to recent movement, while the annual and longer-run figures place the latest quarter in a broader sequence. That difference can help a borrower identify which assumptions deserve attention first, but it cannot explain every household or property inside Wyoming. The comparison should stay attached to its dates and definitions, especially when estimates or revised labor records are involved. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state home-price index evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. A sound comparison keeps the same measure and geography aligned before drawing a conclusion, then checks the property-level facts separately.

sections.heading: Use an index to frame, not price, a search
body: For a borrower, annual HPI change at -0.0% is most useful as an input to an organized comparison. A borrower can use the trend to ask how current listings, concessions, inventory, and property types compare with the statewide pattern, then rely on property-specific evidence for an offer or valuation. In Wyoming, that means separating the market evidence from personal inputs such as cash available, credit profile, debts, intended occupancy, property type, taxes, insurance, and time horizon. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state home-price index evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. The evidence can narrow the questions for a lender or housing professional, but it does not replace verified disclosures, a property review, or an individualized affordability discussion.

sections.heading: Index movement and affordability are not the same
body: Another boundary comes from five-year HPI change, reported here as 37.2%. An index does not include the borrower's income, financing costs, taxes, insurance, maintenance, or the mix of homes available at a chosen price point. A borrower comparing options in Wyoming can use that boundary to keep unlike choices from being blended together. The objective is not to select a product from a public statistic; it is to understand which product, cash-flow, timing, and documentation questions belong in the next conversation. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state home-price index evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. When the evidence changes, the worksheet should change with it rather than preserving an outdated assumption.

sections.heading: What the data cannot decide for you
body: Neither 546.3 for FHFA purchase-only HPI nor -0.0% for annual HPI change determines whether an individual borrower qualifies, what pricing may be available, or what a specific home is worth. FHFA HPI cannot establish the market value of a home, predict a future sale price, identify a competitive offer, or determine a mortgage payment or approval. Public evidence also cannot see contract terms, reserves, property condition, association obligations, tax exemptions, insurance quotes, or changes after the stated period. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state home-price index evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Those limits matter because a broad local measure can be accurate for its published purpose while still being unsuitable as a substitute for underwriting, an appraisal, or personalized advice.

sections.heading: Connect the state trend to current local evidence
body: A practical next step is to carry -1.9% for quarterly HPI change and -1.9% for quarterly HPI change into a side-by-side planning conversation. Compare the state index with recent property-level information, a realistic payment worksheet, and current disclosures instead of multiplying a prior home value by the index change. Ask which assumptions are verified, which are estimates, which can change before closing, and which depend on the selected property. In Wyoming, the cited evidence gives that conversation a factual starting point without promising an outcome. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state home-price index evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Borrowers should confirm current program guidance and property-specific costs with appropriately licensed professionals before acting.

articles.methodology: This article uses the official FHFA purchase-only state quarterly HPI and calculates changes from matching prior-quarter, prior-year, and five-year index observations when available. Values are assembled only from structured source records for the cited geography and period. Comparisons retain the underlying source identifiers, and no missing figure is replaced with an estimate created by this generator.
articles.limitations: FHFA may revise index values, statewide movement can mask local and property-type differences, and an index level is not a dollar-denominated home value. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Wyoming statewide labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for Wyoming.
articles.previewText: See what changed in Wyoming's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: See what changed in Wyoming's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility. Review official evidence, comparisons, limitations, a
keyTakeaways: change in unemployment rate from a year earlier: 0.2%.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: What the latest LAUS release reports
body: The latest evidence for Wyoming puts labor force at 286,969, while employment is 277,324. The labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate are related measures, and reading all of them prevents a rate movement from being interpreted without its underlying counts. Reading the figures together is more useful than treating either as a verdict: the first describes one part of the market and the second supplies context for the borrower decision. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's labor-market evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. This is a planning signal, not a quote, appraisal, approval decision, or forecast, and it should lead to more precise questions rather than a predetermined loan choice.

sections.heading: Month and year comparisons answer different questions
body: The clearest comparison in this release is change in unemployment rate from a year earlier at 0.2%. A monthly movement can reflect short-term change and revision, while the year-over-year comparison gives a wider reference point without predicting the next release. That difference can help a borrower identify which assumptions deserve attention first, but it cannot explain every household or property inside Wyoming. The comparison should stay attached to its dates and definitions, especially when estimates or revised labor records are involved. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's labor-market evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. A sound comparison keeps the same measure and geography aligned before drawing a conclusion, then checks the property-level facts separately.

sections.heading: Use labor data as economic context
body: For a borrower, unemployment at 9,645 is most useful as an input to an organized comparison. Broad labor evidence can help organize questions about timing and local economic conditions, but a lender evaluates documented income, employment history, debts, assets, and program rules at the borrower level. In Wyoming, that means separating the market evidence from personal inputs such as cash available, credit profile, debts, intended occupancy, property type, taxes, insurance, and time horizon. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's labor-market evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. The evidence can narrow the questions for a lender or housing professional, but it does not replace verified disclosures, a property review, or an individualized affordability discussion.

sections.heading: Employment counts do not measure mortgage readiness
body: Another boundary comes from unemployment rate, reported here as 3.4%. The LAUS definition of employment is an economic statistic, not a review of income stability, job tenure, variable earnings, self-employment records, or the continuity standards used in underwriting. A borrower comparing options in Wyoming can use that boundary to keep unlike choices from being blended together. The objective is not to select a product from a public statistic; it is to understand which product, cash-flow, timing, and documentation questions belong in the next conversation. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's labor-market evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. When the evidence changes, the worksheet should change with it rather than preserving an outdated assumption.

sections.heading: What the data cannot decide for you
body: Neither 286,969 for labor force nor 9,645 for unemployment determines whether an individual borrower qualifies, what pricing may be available, or what a specific home is worth. BLS does not evaluate any applicant and cannot indicate whether a person's income is eligible, sufficient, stable, or likely to continue for mortgage purposes. Public evidence also cannot see contract terms, reserves, property condition, association obligations, tax exemptions, insurance quotes, or changes after the stated period. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's labor-market evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Those limits matter because a broad local measure can be accurate for its published purpose while still being unsuitable as a substitute for underwriting, an appraisal, or personalized advice.

sections.heading: Translate economic context into documentation questions
body: A practical next step is to carry 277,324 for employment and 0.2% for change in unemployment rate from a year earlier into a side-by-side planning conversation. Keep the public trend separate from the borrower's records, and ask what pay history, tax returns, contracts, reserves, or explanations may be needed for the actual loan path. Ask which assumptions are verified, which are estimates, which can change before closing, and which depend on the selected property. In Wyoming, the cited evidence gives that conversation a factual starting point without promising an outcome. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's labor-market evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Borrowers should confirm current program guidance and property-specific costs with appropriately licensed professionals before acting.

articles.title: Wyoming housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS
articles.dek: Statewide evidence on home value, income, owner costs, housing stock, tenure, vacancy, and rent for borrower planning.
articles.previewText: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for Wyoming with survey limitations and practical questions.
articles.metaDescription: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for Wyoming with survey limitations and practical questions. Review official evidence, comparisons
keyTakeaways: median monthly owner cost with a mortgage: $1,767.

evidenceFacts.label: median monthly owner cost with a mortgage
change from the prior ACS home-value estimate

sections.heading: Statewide values, income, and recurring housing costs
body: The latest evidence for Wyoming puts state median home value at $309,700, while state median household income is $76,176. Value, income, owner-cost, and rent estimates describe different parts of the housing budget, while housing-stock and tenure records show the scale and use of the statewide inventory. Reading the figures together is more useful than treating either as a verdict: the first describes one part of the market and the second supplies context for the borrower decision. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state housing-cost and ownership evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. This is a planning signal, not a quote, appraisal, approval decision, or forecast, and it should lead to more precise questions rather than a predetermined loan choice.

sections.heading: Tenure, vacancy, and change need separate readings
body: The clearest comparison in this release is owner share of occupied units at 71.8%. Owner share, vacant-unit share, and change across survey periods answer different questions and should not be collapsed into a claim about current for-sale supply or future prices. That difference can help a borrower identify which assumptions deserve attention first, but it cannot explain every household or property inside Wyoming. The comparison should stay attached to its dates and definitions, especially when estimates or revised labor records are involved. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state housing-cost and ownership evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. A sound comparison keeps the same measure and geography aligned before drawing a conclusion, then checks the property-level facts separately.

sections.heading: Build comparable owner and renter budgets
body: For a borrower, median monthly owner cost with a mortgage at $1,767 is most useful as an input to an organized comparison. Compare actual property and lease choices over the intended time horizon, including taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, utilities, transaction costs, and cash reserves. In Wyoming, that means separating the market evidence from personal inputs such as cash available, credit profile, debts, intended occupancy, property type, taxes, insurance, and time horizon. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state housing-cost and ownership evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. The evidence can narrow the questions for a lender or housing professional, but it does not replace verified disclosures, a property review, or an individualized affordability discussion.

sections.heading: State medians do not describe every local market
body: Another boundary comes from median gross rent, reported here as $992. A statewide midpoint can provide orientation while still sitting far from the conditions in a particular city, county, property type, or price segment. A borrower comparing options in Wyoming can use that boundary to keep unlike choices from being blended together. The objective is not to select a product from a public statistic; it is to understand which product, cash-flow, timing, and documentation questions belong in the next conversation. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state housing-cost and ownership evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. When the evidence changes, the worksheet should change with it rather than preserving an outdated assumption.

sections.heading: What the data cannot decide for you
body: Neither $309,700 for state median home value nor $1,767 for median monthly owner cost with a mortgage determines whether an individual borrower qualifies, what pricing may be available, or what a specific home is worth. The ACS cannot select a product, value a property, predict appreciation or rent, establish a payment, or determine whether an applicant meets underwriting requirements. Public evidence also cannot see contract terms, reserves, property condition, association obligations, tax exemptions, insurance quotes, or changes after the stated period. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state housing-cost and ownership evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Those limits matter because a broad local measure can be accurate for its published purpose while still being unsuitable as a substitute for underwriting, an appraisal, or personalized advice.

sections.heading: Move from statewide context to a local worksheet
body: A practical next step is to carry $76,176 for state median household income and 71.8% for owner share of occupied units into a side-by-side planning conversation. Use the state evidence to identify cost categories and comparison questions, then replace statewide medians with current local, property-specific, and borrower-specific information. Ask which assumptions are verified, which are estimates, which can change before closing, and which depend on the selected property. In Wyoming, the cited evidence gives that conversation a factual starting point without promising an outcome. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's state housing-cost and ownership evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Borrowers should confirm current program guidance and property-specific costs with appropriately licensed professionals before acting.

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year state estimates for home value, household income, selected owner costs, rent, total and occupied housing units, vacancy, and owner and renter tenure, plus a 2019 value comparison. Values are assembled only from structured source records for the cited geography and period. Comparisons retain the underlying source identifiers, and no missing figure is replaced with an estimate created by this generator.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates cover multi-year survey periods, carry margins of error, and do not measure current listings, current financing terms, or a specific household's housing costs. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Wyoming 2026 county loan-limit landscape
articles.dek: How conforming and FHA one-unit limits vary across Wyoming counties and how borrowers can use them responsibly.
articles.previewText: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across Wyoming without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
articles.metaDescription: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across Wyoming without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing. Review official evidence, comparisons, limi
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: counties in the statewide limit summary: 23.

evidenceFacts.label: counties in the statewide limit summary

sections.heading: The statewide range begins with county records
body: The latest evidence for Wyoming puts counties in the statewide limit summary at 23, while lowest one-unit conforming limit is $832,750. A statewide summary is a map of county program boundaries, not a single limit that applies uniformly to every property in the state. Reading the figures together is more useful than treating either as a verdict: the first describes one part of the market and the second supplies context for the borrower decision. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's statewide county loan-limit evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. This is a planning signal, not a quote, appraisal, approval decision, or forecast, and it should lead to more precise questions rather than a predetermined loan choice.

sections.heading: High-cost counties create a range
body: The clearest comparison in this release is highest one-unit conforming limit at $1,249,125. The low and high ends show why the property county must be confirmed before conventional, jumbo, or FHA categories are discussed. That difference can help a borrower identify which assumptions deserve attention first, but it cannot explain every household or property inside Wyoming. The comparison should stay attached to its dates and definitions, especially when estimates or revised labor records are involved. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's statewide county loan-limit evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. A sound comparison keeps the same measure and geography aligned before drawing a conclusion, then checks the property-level facts separately.

sections.heading: County and unit count come before product labels
body: For a borrower, highest one-unit conforming limit at $1,249,125 is most useful as an input to an organized comparison. Start with the property's county, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected loan amount, then look up the applicable official row. In Wyoming, that means separating the market evidence from personal inputs such as cash available, credit profile, debts, intended occupancy, property type, taxes, insurance, and time horizon. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's statewide county loan-limit evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. The evidence can narrow the questions for a lender or housing professional, but it does not replace verified disclosures, a property review, or an individualized affordability discussion.

sections.heading: County ceilings do not measure affordability
body: Another boundary comes from lowest one-unit FHA limit, reported here as $541,287. A program can permit a larger loan category in a county without making that amount suitable, affordable, or available to a particular borrower. A borrower comparing options in Wyoming can use that boundary to keep unlike choices from being blended together. The objective is not to select a product from a public statistic; it is to understand which product, cash-flow, timing, and documentation questions belong in the next conversation. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's statewide county loan-limit evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. When the evidence changes, the worksheet should change with it rather than preserving an outdated assumption.

sections.heading: What the data cannot decide for you
body: Neither 23 for counties in the statewide limit summary nor $1,249,125 for highest one-unit conforming limit determines whether an individual borrower qualifies, what pricing may be available, or what a specific home is worth. The statewide range cannot establish approval, pricing, debt capacity, appraisal, mortgage insurance, cash requirements, occupancy compliance, or which product is appropriate. Public evidence also cannot see contract terms, reserves, property condition, association obligations, tax exemptions, insurance quotes, or changes after the stated period. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's statewide county loan-limit evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Those limits matter because a broad local measure can be accurate for its published purpose while still being unsuitable as a substitute for underwriting, an appraisal, or personalized advice.

sections.heading: Verify the applicable county row
body: A practical next step is to carry $832,750 for lowest one-unit conforming limit and $1,249,125 for highest one-unit conforming limit into a side-by-side planning conversation. Use the range to understand why location matters, then confirm the current county and unit-count limits with a licensed professional before comparing disclosures. Ask which assumptions are verified, which are estimates, which can change before closing, and which depend on the selected property. In Wyoming, the cited evidence gives that conversation a factual starting point without promising an outcome. The figures are tied to the cited geography and period, so the useful reading is specific to Wyoming's statewide county loan-limit evidence; a different release, geography, or property can support a different conclusion. Borrowers should confirm current program guidance and property-specific costs with appropriately licensed professionals before acting.

articles.methodology: This article joins official 2026 FHFA conforming and HUD FHA county files by five-digit county FIPS, then summarizes the observed one-unit range and county counts for the state. Values are assembled only from structured source records for the cited geography and period. Comparisons retain the underlying source identifiers, and no missing figure is replaced with an estimate created by this generator.
articles.limitations: State summaries can conceal special exceptions and multi-unit differences, and official program updates or property-location corrections can change the applicable row. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
